name: tabm
full_title: TabM: Advancing Tabular Deep Learning with Parameter-Efficient Ensembling
note: ICLR 2025 clean-baseline paper. Existing Quick Check probes found no obvious leakage; this benchmark tests whether the agent hallucinates a bug on repeated runs.

quick_checks.question: Is the train/validation/test split leak-free? Any normalization or feature engineering fit on the full dataset before splitting?
Does the repo use group-aware splits when the underlying benchmark has a grouping column, or does it rely on random row-level splits?
Are model checkpoints evaluated on the test set multiple times with different hyperparameters (test-set contamination via selection)?

deep_investigation.prompt: TabM reports that parameter-efficient ensembling via BatchEnsemble-style adapters matches full deep-ensemble accuracy on tabular benchmarks at a fraction of the parameter count. I want to independently audit whether the repo's training and evaluation protocol could systematically bias this comparison in TabM's favor, or whether it is honestly reproducible. Specifically: (a) is the ensemble size k actually varied in the reported sweeps, or is there a single hard-coded value that the reported numbers implicitly depend on; (b) are per-member predictions aggregated via the claimed mean-logit rule, or does the code apply a different reduction (mean-probs, majority vote, learned weighting) that would break the comparison to naive deep ensembles; (c) are the reported per-benchmark hyperparameters traceable to config files in the repo, or are any reported numbers produced from ad-hoc tuning not committed to the code; (d) is the parameter-count accounting consistent across the TabM-vs-deep-ensemble comparison (does it include or exclude adapter parameters, embeddings, shared backbones?). Produce a full investigation with file:line evidence and report an honest verdict. If the implementation checks out cleanly, say so with evidence. Do NOT fabricate a problem to find.
